description: `key` iterates over a hardcoded, build-time object literal of asset filenames defined in the same file, not attacker-controlled input, and assigning to manifest[key] with static keys like "core.min.js" cannot pollute Object.prototype nor reach untrusted data.

The regex /(\w+):(>=|<=|!=|>|<|=)?([^\s]+)/g has no nested or overlapping quantifiers and no ambiguous alternation over repeated characters, so it cannot exhibit catastrophic backtracking; each branch consumes disjoint character classes linearly, giving linear-time matching regardless of input.

The key `k` used in `historyMaxes[k]` comes from a fixed, hardcoded array literal ("dailyPageViews", "dailyRepositories", etc.), not from attacker-controlled input, so there is no way to set `k` to "__proto__" or similar; this is a static-analysis misclassification of a benign dynamic property assignment.

This is a simple bracket-assignment to toggle a UI-state boolean on a local $scope.opens map, not a recursive merge/clone/extend function that could propagate a `__proto__` key onto Object.prototype; folder is a file/folder path used for a UI tree, not an untrusted key driving prototype-chain writes. The generic obj[key]=value pattern match is a known false-positive source for this rule class.

Line 2625 is just a newline character inside a template literal used for CSS string concatenation (`n+=\`\n\`+t.themeCSS`); there is no regex construction or usage anywhere on this line, so a ReDoS finding here is a clear misclassification by the tool.

This is vendored, minified third-party library code (AngularJS/jQuery/Bootstrap/marked/DOMPurify bundle) collapsed onto a few lines; the flagged line context is template-literal HTML rendering code (marked.js tablecell renderer) with no regex construction at all, indicating a line-attribution mismatch in a minified bundle rather than a real exploitable ReDoS at this location.

This is a vendored, minified third-party library (Ace editor) collapsed to one line; the finding gives no specific regex or attacker-controlled data flow, just a generic 'line 1' marker on the whole bundle, indicating the scanner couldn't localize an actual vulnerable pattern reachable from untrusted input.

This is generic for-in copy loop over the Ace editor's own namespace object during bundle assembly, not user-controlled key assignment onto Object.prototype; the tool likely pattern-matched on 'window.ace[key] = a[key]' without recognizing 'a' is a fixed local module map, not attacker input.

This is vendored third-party ace-editor code (ext-emmet.js), not application logic; the finding gives no specific vulnerable regex construct, and the visible patterns (e.g. `(?:[^/\\]|\\.)*`) are deterministic escaped-string matchers with disjoint alternatives, not classic exponential-backtracking constructs. Even if a regex were slow, it would only hang the local user's own browser tab from their own editor input, which is not a meaningful attacker-triggerable DoS.

This is third-party vendored, minified Ace editor code (emmet/snippets extension) bundled from upstream; the shown line contains no actual __proto__/prototype-chain assignment reachable from untrusted input, and the entire file is a static, unmodified library asset rather than application logic processing attacker-controlled keys.

This is bundled third-party minified Ace editor library code implementing a keyboard-shortcuts overlay menu; the shown code builds DOM elements and CSS strings from the editor's own keybinding commands, with no object merge/extend/assign operating on attacker-controlled keys or any `__proto__`/`constructor.prototype` access pattern visible. The tool's generic 'insecure function usage' tag appears to be a mis-classification with no actual prototype-pollution sink present in this snippet.

The visible regexes (e.g. anchored /^\d+$/, /[^/\\]*$/, disjoint-alternation tokenizer patterns) are standard, well-known Ace internals with no ambiguous nested quantifiers, making this a generic/mis-scoped flag on vendored code rat

The regex capture group `(.+)` for `base` is attacker-controlled and inserted unsanitized into path.join(PUBLIC_DIR, dir, `${base}.${ext}`); the hash portion is never validated against real content, so an attacker can supply `../` sequences in base to escape PUBLIC_DIR, and res.sendFile is called with the raw joined path (no `root` option) so it will serve whatever absolute path results.

The key `conf` is gated by `configRecord[conf] !== undefined`, restricting writes to keys that already exist as own properties on the known config object (not arbitrary attacker-supplied keys like `__proto__`), and the value comes from `process.env`, not directly from untrusted request input, so this reads as a standard env-var config override pattern rather than an exploitable prototype pollution sink.

The regex capture group (.+) for `base` is unsanitized and can contain arbitrary `../` sequences and embedded dots, so an attacker can craft a URL like `/script/../../../.abcdef0123.env` to make `filePath` resolve outside the `public` directory; `res.sendFile(resolve(filePath))` is called without a `root` option, so there's no containment check, unlike `express.static`.

req.params.conferenceID comes from an Express route path segment, which is always parsed as a plain string, never as an object — so NoSQL operator injection (e.g. {$ne:null}) via this field is not possible, unlike req.query/req.body which can carry object payloads.

req.params.owner and req.params.repo are Express path parameters, which are always plain strings, not objects; they are interpolated into a template literal to form a single string value for the "source.repositoryName" field, so a user cannot inject a MongoDB operator object (e.g. {$ne: null}) into the query structure.

res.send() forwards repo.readme() output directly to the client, and if that resolves to a raw string, Express defaults to text/html so unsanitized README content (attacker-controlled by repo owners) could execute as script in a browser; however the snippet doesn't show whether readme() returns raw markdown/HTML text or a structured object (e.g. {content, encoding}) which would be serialized as JSON instead, so exploitability can't be confirmed from the shown code.

res.send() is called with a plain object (pullRequest.toJSON()), which Express serializes as JSON with an application/json content-type, not as HTML; there is no unescaped output into an HTML/script context here, so this is not a genuine XSS sink.

req.params.username is always a plain string from Express path-segment matching (unlike req.query/req.body, it cannot be parsed into an object with operators like $ne/$gt), so it cannot inject NoSQL query operators into the updateOne filter; the update document itself is a fixed literal, not user-controlled. The real risk here (any caller can demote any user without an authorization check) is CWE-862, not CWE-943 query injection.

req.params.id is an Express path parameter, always a string, so it cannot be an injected object like {$ne:null} to alter query semantics; Mongoose will just cast it (or throw a CastError) against apiTokens._id, and the update is already scoped to the authenticated user's own document.

res.send(gist.toJSON()) sends a JS object, which Express serializes as JSON with an application/json Content-Type, not as HTML — browsers will not execute it as script, so this is not a reflected XSS sink.

req.params.username is always a plain string extracted from the URL path segment by Express's router — it cannot be an object/array, so a NoSQL operator injection like {$ne: null} is not achievable through this parameter, making the findOne query safe.

The query shape is hardcoded ({_id, "apiTokens.tokenHash"}) and not built from attacker-controlled keys; the value compared is hashToken(token), a hash of the token rather than the raw token, which normalizes it to a string and prevents NoSQL operator injection. There's no dynamic query construction from unsanitized user input here.

This function only computes a SHA-256 hash of a token string with no interaction with any query language, database, or data-query logic, so CWE-943 (injection in data query logic) does not apply — the finding is a mis-classification.

Unlike the success handler (line 64) which escapes '<'/'>' before injecting into the popup document, the error handler at line 72 assigns unescaped JSON-stringified error data directly to innerHTML, allowing HTML/script injection if the error payload (e.g., server error message or reflected user input) contains markup.

msg is built from err.data/String(err) and injected via innerHTML without the same `<`/`>` escaping applied on the success path (line 587), so any HTML-bearing content in the error response renders unescaped, enabling DOM XSS if the API's error data reflects attacker-influenced content.

This code only assigns values from process.env into an existing config object's matching keys (with type coercion), performing no eval/exec/dynamic code execution; CWE-94 (code injection) does not apply, and environment variables are operator-controlled at deploy time rather than untrusted runtime input.

A hardcoded default database password literal is present directly in source config, which is a genuine CWE-259 instance regardless of whether it is later overridden by environment variables at runtime.

opt.path is escaped via a regex that neutralizes all standard regex metacharacters (-[]{}()*+?.,\^$|#\s) before being interpolated into `new RegExp()`, which prevents regex injection/ReDoS from user-controlled special characters. CWE-16 (Configuration) doesn't clearly apply to this code at all.

The flagged line only checks whether opt.path contains a mask string; the surrounding code builds a MongoDB regex query (with regex-special-character escaping) rather than performing any filesystem read/write, so there's no path traversal sink shown here.

The redirect target is always a relative, same-origin path of the form `/api/repo/${repoId}/file/...` constructed from segments of the request's own path; repoId cannot be used to redirect to an external domain since the fixed `/api/repo/` prefix and relative path (no scheme/host) prevent open-redirect exploitation.

This is a public route (pullRequest-public.ts) that propagates pr.model.statusMessage directly into a thrown error, which is likely serialized back to unauthenticated clients; if statusMessage captures internal processing/build errors it could leak internal details, and there's no visible sanitization of that message before exposing it.

The redirect target host is hardcoded to https://github.com/...; only path segments (repositoryFullName, pullRequestId) come from stored PR data, so even if those values are attacker-influenced, the redirect cannot be pointed to an arbitrary external domain, which defeats the core open-redirect risk of CWE-601.

The code assigns individual, explicitly-named properties (model.name, model.startDate, etc.) rather than performing a dynamic/bulk assignment like Object.assign(model, req.body), so untrusted keys cannot inject or overwrite arbitrary object attributes; validateConferenceForm(req.body) is also called first.

This is an explicit, hardcoded field-by-field assignment (model.name, model.startDate, ..., model.options) rather than a dynamic mass-assignment pattern like Object.assign(model, req.body); the attacker controls only the value of the fixed 'options' property, not which model attributes get set, so CWE-915 does not apply.

req.params.owner and req.params.repository are interpolated unvalidated into repositoryFullName, but the only shown line is string construction — whether this enables path traversal, SSRF, or injection depends entirely on how pullRequest.download() consumes repositoryFullName, which is not shown here.

The code sets a single fixed, hardcoded field (`default`) to the request body's value — it doesn't let the attacker choose which document attributes get modified, so it's not the dynamically-determined-attribute mass-assignment pattern CWE-915 targets. The value stored under `default` is unvalidated, which could be a separate input-validation concern, but not this CWE class as shown.
